637e966ad626499cd9592d77.question: Cy Twombly used a white crayon to scratch out the name of this town in a 1967 “blackboard painting” that was inspired by an author whom he claimed was impossible to not love after coming out of Black Mountain College. The speaker of a poem titled for this town asks the Earth if it dreams “to be wholly invisible someday” and earlier argues that humans long for destiny “not because happiness exists” but because (+) “truly being here is so much.” Ludwig Boltzmann committed suicide while vacationing in this town. While walking along a cliffside in this city, one author claimed that a voice in the wind shouted a line that he then wrote down in his notebook. A poem named for this town ends with the declaration, (*) we “would feel the emotion, that almost dismays us, when a joyful thing falls.” For 10 points, name this Italian resort city that inspired the line “who if I cried out would hear me among the angelic orders?” in an elegy by Rainer Maria Rilke.

637e966ad626499cd9592d7c.question: In a short poem, this poet quips that the word intellectual “suggests right away / A man who’s untrue to his wife.” In a different short poem, this poet wrote that, “Money cannot buy / the fuel of love / but is excellent kindling.” The speaker of another poem by this author proposes that law is a desire to “slip out of our own position / Into an unconcerned condition” and suggests it should be “Like love we often weep / Like love we seldom keep.” In a poem by this author of “Law Like Love,” the speaker hears someone declare, “The years shall run like rabbits” because they hold (+) “The Flower of the Ages / and the first love of the world.” This author ponders stars burning “with a passion for us we could not return” in a poem whose speaker declares (*) “If equal affection cannot be / Let the more loving one be me.” This author writes “the lovers they were gone / the clocks had ceased their chiming / And the deep river ran on” to conclude a poem about a stroll “down Bristol Street.” For 10 points, what poet of “As I Walked Out One Evening” wrote the line “We must love another or die?”
637e966ad626499cd9592d7c.question_sanitized: In a short poem, this poet quips that the word intellectual "suggests right away / A man who's untrue to his wife." In a different short poem, this poet wrote that, "Money cannot buy / the fuel of love / but is excellent kindling." The speaker of another poem by this author proposes that law is a desire to "slip out of our own position / Into an unconcerned condition" and suggests it should be "Like love we often weep / Like love we seldom keep." In a poem by this author of "Law Like Love," the speaker hears someone declare, "The years shall run like rabbits" because they hold (+) "The Flower of the Ages / and the first love of the world." This author ponders stars burning "with a passion for us we could not return" in a poem whose speaker declares (*) "If equal affection cannot be / Let the more loving one be me." This author writes "the lovers they were gone / the clocks had ceased their chiming / And the deep river ran on" to conclude a poem about a stroll "down Bristol Street." For 10 points, what poet of "As I Walked Out One Evening" wrote the line "We must love another or die?"
637e966ad626499cd9592d7c.answer_sanitized: W. H. Auden [or Wystan Hugh Auden]

637e966ad626499cd9592d7f.question: Youri Vámos reworked this ballet into a 1992 production about Anna Anderson’s false memories as Anastasia Romanov. Quick solo dances marked Candide and Canari appear in the pas de six in this ballet’s prologue. In an extremely hard move in this ballet, the lead ballerina is required to stand en pointe on one leg as she takes the hand of four men, who each turn her in a (+) circle. An act of this ballet opens with a grand waltz performed by male and female dancers carrying garlands. A harp cadenza opens a dance in this ballet in which the female lead is handed flowers by four (*) suitors. This ballet ends with a grand wedding whose guests include fairy tale characters like the Bluebird and Puss in Boots. A birthday party in this ballet is the setting for the Rose Adagio. For 10 points, name this ballet choreographed by Marius Petipa in which Prince Désiré lifts a hundred-year curse inflicted on the title girl, created by Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky.

An act of this ballet opens with a grand waltz performed by male and female dancers carrying garlands. A harp cadenza opens a dance in this ballet in which the female lead is handed flowers by four (*) suitors. This ballet ends with a grand wedding whose guests include fairy tale characters like the Bluebird and Puss in Boots. A birthday party in this ballet is the setting for the Rose Adagio. For 10 points, name this ballet choreographed by Marius Petipa in which Prince Desire lifts a hundred-year curse inflicted on the title girl, created by Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky.

637e966ad626499cd9592d80.question: In Muhammad and the Empires of Faith, Sean Anthony argued that the story of this poet’s life was based directly on accounts of Muhammad laid out by Ibn Ishaq. The use of the word “scōp” in a work by this poet has led to debates over whether the central figure is creating the world analogously to poetry. This poet’s most famous work ends by describing how the (+) Lord created “Heaven as a roof” and “the middle-earth, the Guardian of mankind” for humanity, and it opens by declaring “Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom.” According to tradition, this poet composed his most famous work in a (*) dream vision while sleeping in a stable at Hild of Whitby’s monastery. In the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, the Venerable Bede claimed that this poet was an illiterate and musically untrained cow-herd who was then blessed with divine inspiration. For 10 points, name this Anglo-Saxon poet, the author of a hymn that is the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry.

This poet's most famous work ends by describing how the (+) Lord created "Heaven as a roof" and "the middle-earth, the Guardian of mankind" for humanity, and it opens by declaring "Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom." According to tradition, this poet composed his most famous work in a (*) dream vision while sleeping in a stable at Hild of Whitby's monastery. In the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, the Venerable Bede claimed that this poet was an illiterate and musically untrained cow-herd who was then blessed with divine inspiration. For 10 points, name this Anglo-Saxon poet, the author of a hymn that is the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry.

637e966ad626499cd9592d8c.question: Note to players: A specific Greek word, or the Anglicized version of it, is required. In his tract against eating meat, Porphyry (PAR-feer-ee) distinguishes an elected group of these people called the Samanaeans from their hereditary counterparts. When Onesicretis (oh-nee-SEE-cree-tis) ventured into a forest to tell one of these people that the “son of Zeus” desired an audience, and threatened him with beheading if he refused, Onesicretis received the reply that “We do not love gold nor fear death.” Plutarch writes that one of these people gave the answer (+) “Day, by one day” to the question “which is older, day or night?” during an incident in which ten of them were accused of inciting a mutiny. Examples of these people include Dandamis (DAN-dah-mis), who lived near Taxila (TAX-ill-ah), and Calanus (KAH-lah-nus), who told Alexander that the two would later meet in Babylon before immolating himself. Greek and Latin accounts of these people closely reflect the practices of (*) mendicant sramanas (SHRAH-mun-nus) and ascetic Jain saints. For 10 points, give this Greek word for philosophers and teachers who did not wear clothing.
637e966ad626499cd9592d8c.question_sanitized: Note to players: A specific Greek word, or the Anglicized version of it, is required. In his tract against eating meat, Porphyry (PAR-feer-ee) distinguishes an elected group of these people called the Samanaeans from their hereditary counterparts. When Onesicretis (oh-nee-SEE-cree-tis) ventured into a forest to tell one of these people that the "son of Zeus" desired an audience, and threatened him with beheading if he refused, Onesicretis received the reply that "We do not love gold nor fear death." Plutarch writes that one of these people gave the answer (+) "Day, by one day" to the question "which is older, day or night?" during an incident in which ten of them were accused of inciting a mutiny. Examples of these people include Dandamis (DAN-dah-mis), who lived near Taxila (TAX-ill-ah), and Calanus (KAH-lah-nus), who told Alexander that the two would later meet in Babylon before immolating himself. Greek and Latin accounts of these people closely reflect the practices of (*) mendicant sramanas (SHRAH-mun-nus) and ascetic Jain saints. For 10 points, give this Greek word for philosophers and teachers who did not wear clothing.
637e966ad626499cd9592d8c.answer: gymnosophists [or gymnosophistes or gymnosophistai; prompt on sramanas before “sramanas”; prompt on philosophers before “philosophers”; prompt on teachers or swamis or gurus or sadhus; reject “sophists”]

637e966ad626499cd9592d99.question: Members of this group sing a refrain asking, “What is wisdom?” in part of a song that opens, “O when will I be dancing, leaping barefoot through the night?” Near the beginning of the work they appear in, nine members of this group discuss how the Curetes protected a baby Zeus and compare it to the birth of a figure they call “Bromius.” In that work, an “old gray head” asks another old man to be his (+) nursemaid to guide him in joining this group. A member of this group is asked to remember being wed in the house of Echion (ee-KYE-an) after being instructed to look to the sky and look to the inner spirit. In the play they appear in, a messenger travels to a palace destroyed by (*) lightning to relate how this group threw rocks and branches at a man hiding at the top of a fir tree. This group carries the head of a king back to Thebes after killing him under the leadership of Autonoe, Ino, and Agave. For 10 points, name this group that titles the final play written by Euripides, in which they dismember Pentheus while under the power of Dionysus.

In that work, an "old gray head" asks another old man to be his (+) nursemaid to guide him in joining this group. A member of this group is asked to remember being wed in the house of Echion (ee-KYE-an) after being instructed to look to the sky and look to the inner spirit. In the play they appear in, a messenger travels to a palace destroyed by (*) lightning to relate how this group threw rocks and branches at a man hiding at the top of a fir tree. This group carries the head of a king back to Thebes after killing him under the leadership of Autonoe, Ino, and Agave. For 10 points, name this group that titles the final play written by Euripides, in which they dismember Pentheus while under the power of Dionysus.
637e966ad626499cd9592d99.answer: the Bacchae [accept the Bacchantes; I suppose accept other words for followers of Dionysus, such as maenads, bassarides, dithyrambeans, etc.; prompt on the chorus by asking, “What group makes up the chorus?”] (Quotes were taken from Ian Johnston’s translation of Bacchae and the translation provided by gutenberg.org.)

637e966ad626499cd9592d9e.question: Note to players: author and type of work required. The original paper containing one of these works about an “ungovernable lust” sold for a record-breaking 240,000 pounds at auction in 2004. One of these works describes “big fat fellows, long windy ones” before imagining a skinny woman having fun “in a school dormitory at night.” One of these works laments, “No man is worthy of a woman’s love” and closes by quoting the author’s poem “Sleep Now, O Sleep Now.” A biography by Brenda Maddox describes how the most popular of these works were written at the behest of J. F. Byrne while its author was away from his home in (+) Trieste. An early one of these works describes how the author masturbated after a walk through Ringsend with a “naughty shameless girl.” A particularly bad one of these works expresses the author’s love of a woman with “sudden and dry and dirty” (*) farts that he wishes to fuck out of her. These works were addressed to Nora Barnacle, the inspiration for characters such as Gretta Conroy. For 10 points, name these ridiculously filthy epistolary works written by the author of Dubliners.

637e966ad626499cd9592da4.question: In an early comedy by this author, the protagonist suffers delusions of hell after forging love letters in order to prevent the marriage of his beloved and his friend Tircis. A comedy by this author ends with a monologue calling theatre “a fief whose income is good” as two characters look on at a group of actors divvying up money. The protagonist of a play by this author accidentally confesses his feelings for his true love to the princess (+) Rosine before being reprimanded by his true love and then killed by Prince Florilame. The name of the main character switches to Calisto and Theogenes between acts in a 2002 adaptation of one of this author’s plays by Tony Kushner. That comedy ends with a cave-inhabiting (*) magician revealing that characters such as Clindor and Isabelle were actually just actors in a play-within-a-play. This author of The Comic Illusion briefly withdrew from public life after Cardinal Richelieu said that one of his plays too strongly violated the Aristotelian unities. For 10 points, name this French playwright of Le Cid.

That comedy ends with a cave-inhabiting (*) magician revealing that characters such as Clindor and Isabelle were actually just actors in a play-within-a-play. This author of The Comic Illusion briefly withdrew from public life after Cardinal Richelieu said that one of his plays too strongly violated the Aristotelian unities. For 10 points, name this French playwright of Le Cid.
637e966ad626499cd9592da4.answer: Pierre Corneille (The play in the first line is Mélite.)
637e966ad626499cd9592da4.answer_sanitized: Pierre Corneille (The play in the first line is Melite.)

637e966ad626499cd9592da5.question: This man first came to prominence in America after starring as McGafferty in Archibald Macleish’s play Panic. Thornton Wilder’s The Long Christmas Dinner inspired a scene in one of this director’s films in which different conversations between a man and woman are intercut with windows rushing by. This man shot short film sequences for a production of William Gillette’s play (+) Too Much Johnson. Canada Lee starred as Bigger Thomas in a Broadway production of Native Son directed by this man. This man rearranged dialogue from different plays to create the play Five Kings. In collaboration with John Houseman, this director cast Eric Burroughs as a bullwhip-wielding (*) Hecate in an all-black Shakespeare adaptation referred to as the Voodoo Macbeth. Cinna the Poet is killed by fascist secret police in this man’s 1937 adaptation of Julius Caesar put on by the Mercury Theatre. Some people believed a Martian invasion was taking place while listening to this man’s Halloween broadcast of The War of the Worlds. For 10 points, name this man who also directed Citizen Kane.

This man rearranged dialogue from different plays to create the play Five Kings. In collaboration with John Houseman, this director cast Eric Burroughs as a bullwhip-wielding (*) Hecate in an all-black Shakespeare adaptation referred to as the Voodoo Macbeth. Cinna the Poet is killed by fascist secret police in this man's 1937 adaptation of Julius Caesar put on by the Mercury Theatre. Some people believed a Martian invasion was taking place while listening to this man's Halloween broadcast of The War of the Worlds. For 10 points, name this man who also directed Citizen Kane.
637e966ad626499cd9592da5.answer: Orson Welles [or George Orson Welles] (Five Kings was adapted into Chimes at Midnight.)
637e966ad626499cd9592da5.answer_sanitized: Orson Welles [or George Orson Welles] (Five Kings was adapted into Chimes at Midnight.)

637e966ad626499cd9592da8.question: In one section of an epic work by this writer, human bodies are woven by looms in a city built from the primordial Furnaces. A short poem by this writer follows a woman who “hid in Leutha’s (ley-OO-thaz) Vale” and is sexually assaulted before consummating her marriage to Theotormon (thee-oh-TOR-mon). This writer stated, “I must create a system or be enslaved by another man’s” in an epic poem that describes the city of Golgonooza and the marriage of the muse (+) Enitharmon (ee-nih-THAR-mon). This writer retold his Book of Ahania from the point of view of a fallen smith in his Book of Los (rhymes with “dose”). Aphorisms like “The fox provides for himself, but God provides for the lion” and “Exuberance is beauty” are included in a prophetic book by this writer that begins with the (*) roaring of Rintrah. This writer described how the gods Urthona (ur-THOH-nah), Tharmas, Luvah, and Urizen (you-REE-zen) are emanations of a primordial giant in the book The Four Zoas. For 10 points, name this author who created his own mythology about the downfall of Albion that figures into his epic poems Jerusalem and Milton.
637e966ad626499cd9592da8.question_sanitized: In one section of an epic work by this writer, human bodies are woven by looms in a city built from the primordial Furnaces. A short poem by this writer follows a woman who "hid in Leutha's (ley-OO-thaz) Vale" and is sexually assaulted before consummating her marriage to Theotormon (thee-oh-TOR-mon). This writer stated, "I must create a system or be enslaved by another man's" in an epic poem that describes the city of Golgonooza and the marriage of the muse (+) Enitharmon (ee-nih-THAR-mon). This writer retold his Book of Ahania from the point of view of a fallen smith in his Book of Los (rhymes with "dose"). Aphorisms like "The fox provides for himself, but God provides for the lion" and "Exuberance is beauty" are included in a prophetic book by this writer that begins with the (*) roaring of Rintrah. This writer described how the gods Urthona (ur-THOH-nah), Tharmas, Luvah, and Urizen (you-REE-zen) are emanations of a primordial giant in the book The Four Zoas. For 10 points, name this author who created his own mythology about the downfall of Albion that figures into his epic poems Jerusalem and Milton.
637e966ad626499cd9592da8.answer: William Blake (The second sentence refers to the Visions of the Daughters of Albion; the fifth sentence refers to The Marriage of Heaven and Hell.)
637e966ad626499cd9592da8.answer_sanitized: William Blake (The second sentence refers to the Visions of the Daughters of Albion; the fifth sentence refers to The Marriage of Heaven and Hell.)

637e966ad626499cd9592db1.question: At the end of a film named for this song, a migraine-afflicted Richard Widmark character leads a chant of "give 'em hell" after hearing the contents of a note written by the deceased Doc Jones. The music to this song was secured by Richard Wallach after hearing "Gendarmes' Duet" from Offenbach's Genevieve de Brabant in Paris. A popular addition to this song describes the title group finding "the Vikings and the Norse" after traveling "beneath the midnight sun" before describing how the "Iceland girls were fair and slim." According to legend, this song's lyrics were written by an (+) infantryman in Mexico by transposing parts of a flag. This song ends by claiming that the title group can be seen guarding the streets of heaven and earlier claims that the title group first “fight for right and freedom." This song opens by anachronistically commemorating the (*) Battle of Chapultepec and the First Barbary War with the lyric "From the Halls of Montezuma to the shores of Tripoli." For 10 points, name this oldest official song in the U.S. armed forces, whose title branch fights "in the air, on land, and sea."

For 10 points, name this oldest official song in the U.S. armed forces, whose title branch fights "in the air, on land, and sea."
637e966ad626499cd9592db1.answer: Marine's Hymn [accept Marine's fight song, anthem, or similar answers; accept The Halls of Montezuma before read; reject all other military branches]
637e966ad626499cd9592db1.answer_sanitized: Marine's Hymn [accept Marine's fight song, anthem, or similar answers; accept The Halls of Montezuma before read; reject all other military branches]

637e966ad626499cd9592dbf.question: Sections such as “Worship,” “Love,” and “Patriotism” make up part of this author’s song collection called the “Garden of Songs,” which also includes four dance-dramas written by this author. A hymn by this author opines, “What beauty, what shades, what affection, what tenderness” at a figure whose “words from thy lips” are “like nectar to my ears.” As a sixteen-year-old, this author began an early collection of songs in the artificial language of (+) Brajabuli that were eventually published under the name Bhanusimha. A poem by this author celebrates how “The East and the West join together…and weave the garland of love” in the second of five stanzas that end by repeating the phrase “Jaya he.” This author invented six new (*) talas for singing some of over two thousand songs attributed to him, which are collectively known as this author’s “Sangeet.” A patriotic hymn by this author is addressed to the “dispenser” of his home country’s “destiny,” and ends with the exhortation “Victory, victory, victory to thee.” For 10 points, name this author whose songs include the national anthems Amar Shonar Bangla and Jana Gana Mana.

637e966ad626499cd9592dc1.question: In one poem, this poet describes herself and a lover who deceive a “mooning world” with “how prettily we sigh and swear,” saying “And though we lie forever enemies, / Shall rank with Abélard and Héloïse.” In another poem by this author, she warns that after two lovers swear devotion, “Lady, make a note of this: / One of you is lying.” This poet asks “Why is it no one ever sent me yet / One perfect limousine, do you suppose?” before lamenting that it’s always her luck to get (+) “one perfect rose.” In one poem, this poet catalogs “Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt” as “Four of the things I’d rather be without.” A speaker created by this poet asks, “What earthly good can come of it?” after remarking, “Woman lives but in her lord; (*) Count to ten, and man is bored.” This poet who included “Inventory” and “General Review of the Sex Situation” in her collection Enough Rope quipped, “Men seldom make passes / At girls who wear glasses.” For 10 points, “News Item” was written by what heteropessimist poet of the Algonquin Round Table?

In one poem, this poet catalogs "Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt" as "Four of the things I'd rather be without." A speaker created by this poet asks, "What earthly good can come of it?" after remarking, "Woman lives but in her lord; (*) Count to ten, and man is bored." This poet who included "Inventory" and "General Review of the Sex Situation" in her collection Enough Rope quipped, "Men seldom make passes / At girls who wear glasses." For 10 points, "News Item" was written by what heteropessimist poet of the Algonquin Round Table?
637e966ad626499cd9592dc1.answer: Dorothy Parker [or Dorothy Rothschild] (The first three poems in order are: “The Immortals,” “Unfortunate Coincidence,” and “One Perfect Rose.”)

637e966ad626499cd9592dca.question: The author of this poem replaced its use of italics with parentheticals following its initial publication in the collection Songs of the Art Catholic. The author’s friend Thomas Gordon Hake objected to this poem’s unscientific imagery calling Earth a “fretful midge” spinning in the void. 637e966ad626499cd9592dcc.question: This passage likens one figure’s gaze to a dark mist which arises from a cloud in hot weather after that figure is wounded in the belly with a spear and screams with the voice of nine or ten thousand men. After an arrow is pulled from his shoulder, a man in this passage prays for the strength to smite a foe who boasts that he shall never see the sunlight. It begins as a man’s helmet and shield are kindled with a divine flame, likened to the shining of Sirius at harvest-time. This passage is interrupted by a speech which recalls how (+) Otus and Ephialtes imprisoned Ares and how Amphitryon shot Hera in the breast, delivered by Dione (die-OH-nee) to console her wounded child. The central figure of this passage is given a divine vision by Athena which lets him distinguish (*) gods from mortals and is told to not harm any gods except Aphrodite, who protects the Trojans. For 10 points, identify this passage that takes up most of Book V (five) and part of Book VI (six) of the Iliad and celebrates the greatest feats of a king of Argos?

637e966ad626499cd9592dcc.answer: the aristeia (AIR-ih-STEE-ah) of Diomedes [or the aristeia of Tydides; accept Book V of the Iliad before “Book V”; accept answers which refer to Diomedes and fighting gods before “gods”]

In another film, this actor (+) mimes a baseball game alone after showing up to film the Yankees on a day they were playing an away game. In one film, one of this actor’s characters makes his girlfriend hide in a sack full of boots before stealing a vehicle that was earlier stolen by Union army spies. 637e966ad626499cd9592ddf.question: In one poem in this form, the speaker remarks how the stars bring about “The nightès (NIGHT-es) car” at a time when “the waves work less and less.” The depressed speaker complains of “hap” stealing away all his joys in a poem in this form titled for the “strongest stay” of his madness, “The Pillar Perish’d.” Another poem in this form ends with the speaker deploring “rotten boughs to climb” after being taught “perfect wealth” from “Senec and Plato.” A poem beginning (+) “Farewell love” was collected alongside many poems in this form written by Henry Howard in the anthology Tottel’s Miscellany. The speaker sees the phrase “Noli me tangere” written on the (*) collar of an animal in a poem in this form; that poem in this form is believed to be about the author’s potential lover, Anne Boleyn, and is titled “Whoso List to Hunt.” For 10 points, name this poetic form introduced to England by Thomas Wyatt, who translated many poems by Petrarch.

637e966ad626499cd9592de0.question: In a play named after this place, a woman’s escape from a locked room through a hole to the gutter prompts a character to remark, “A wise man for love will seek every hole.” This place was the site of the Mermaid Tavern, where writers such as Ben Jonson and Francis Beaumont met as members of the “Fraternity of Sireniacal (sih-REN-ee-AH-kal) Gentlemen.” The “Bow bells” that speak to Dick Whittington in Dick Whittington and His Cat originate from a real church along this street, the (+) St Mary-le-Bow. For 10 points, name this London thoroughfare that, in a Thomas Middleton comedy, is home to a “Chaste Maid.”

637e966ad626499cd9592e25.question: Roland Barthes (ROH-lahn BART) contrasted this genre with the “Zhdanovian impasse” of theses and propaganda, claiming that it does not seek to “express reality” but to “signify it.” In another essay, this genre is compared to a man who explains to a crowd how a traffic accident occurred, allowing the bystanders who did not witness it to still form a judgment. This genre “eliminated the Aristotelian catharsis, the purging of the emotions through empathy with the stirring fate of the hero” through the creation of “untragic heroes” according to an essay by (+) Walter (VAHL-tuh) Benjamin (BEN-yah-meen). Characters in this genre break down the Not / But element by embodying social commentary with the gestus technique. Audiences may be forced to view works in this genre as learning tools and not pure entertainment through acts such as changing costumes on stage as part of the (*) estrangement effect. Paradigmatic examples of this genre include The Good Person of Szechwan and The Caucasian Chalk Circle. For 10 points, name this theatrical genre developed by Berthold Brecht, which emphasizes grand historical forms.
